expanded_description: A clear introduction to bacterial metabolism for secondary school biology. This covers how bacteria get energy and carbon to grow—from sunlight, from chemicals, and from breaking down organic matter. It explains the different ways bacteria make ATP, how they use oxygen or other molecules to breathe, and why some bacteria can live in extreme environments.

core_concept.principle: Bacteria need two things to grow: an energy source and a carbon source. Some use sunlight for energy (phototrophs), others use chemicals (chemotrophs). Some get carbon from CO₂ (autotrophs), others from organic compounds (heterotrophs). The combination of energy and carbon sources defines four nutritional types.
core_concept.explanation:
All living things need energy to grow and carbon to build their bodies. Animals get both by eating other organisms. Plants get energy from sunlight and carbon from CO₂ in the air. Bacteria are much more versatile. They can use almost anything as an energy source—sunlight, sugar, sulfur, iron, even ammonia.

Bacteria are classified by how they get energy and carbon. Phototrophs use light for energy. Chemotrophs use chemicals (like sugar, hydrogen, or iron). Autotrophs get carbon from CO₂ (like plants). Heterotrophs get carbon from organic compounds (like animals). The four combinations give four nutritional types:

Photoautotrophs: light energy + CO₂ carbon. Examples: cyanobacteria (blue-green algae). They do photosynthesis like plants and produce oxygen.

Photoheterotrophs: light energy + organic carbon. They use light for energy but still need organic compounds for carbon. Examples: purple non-sulfur bacteria.

Chemoautotrophs: chemical energy + CO₂ carbon. They get energy from inorganic chemicals (like ammonia, hydrogen sulfide, or iron) and use CO₂ to build their cells. Examples: nitrifying bacteria (convert ammonia to nitrate), sulfur-oxidizing bacteria (live near volcanic vents).

Chemoheterotrophs: chemical energy + organic carbon. This is the most common type. They break down organic compounds (like sugar) for both energy and carbon. Examples: E. coli, most pathogens, decomposers.

Understanding bacterial metabolism helps us understand how they live in every environment on Earth—from deep-sea vents to your gut. It also helps us use bacteria to make antibiotics, clean up pollution, and produce food.

image.alt: A diagram showing the four nutritional types: photoautotrophs (sunlight + CO₂), photoheterotrophs (sunlight + organic carbon), chemoautotrophs (chemicals + CO₂), chemoheterotrophs (organic compounds)
image.caption: Bacterial nutrition: Photoautotrophs (light + CO₂), Photoheterotrophs (light + organic C), Chemoautotrophs (chemicals + CO₂), Chemoheterotrophs (organic compounds). Bacteria use all four strategies.

sections.section_title: Introduction: How Bacteria Make a Living
sections.introduction:
Bacteria are everywhere—in soil, water, and inside your body. They live in hot springs, deep-sea vents, and Antarctic ice. How do they survive in so many different places? The answer is metabolic diversity.

All organisms need two things to grow: energy and carbon. Energy is needed to do work (like moving, growing, and making molecules). Carbon is needed to build the structures of the cell (like proteins, DNA, and cell walls). Animals get both by eating other organisms. Plants get energy from sunlight and carbon from CO₂. Bacteria have many more options.

Some bacteria are photosynthetic—they use sunlight for energy, like plants. Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) are photoautotrophs: they use sunlight for energy and CO₂ for carbon. They produce oxygen as a byproduct. Billions of years ago, cyanobacteria changed the Earth's atmosphere by pumping out oxygen.

Some bacteria get energy from inorganic chemicals. These are chemoautotrophs. They live in places where sunlight does not reach—deep-sea vents, deep in the soil, inside rocks. They use hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), ammonia (NH₃), or iron (Fe²⁺) as energy sources. They get carbon from CO₂. They are the basis of food chains in places without sunlight.

Most bacteria are chemoheterotrophs. They get both energy and carbon from organic compounds—the same way animals do. They break down sugars, fats, and proteins. Some are decomposers, breaking down dead plants and animals. Some are pathogens, infecting living hosts. Some live in your gut, helping you digest food.

Bacteria also have different ways of making ATP (the energy currency of the cell). Some use oxygen (aerobic respiration) and get a lot of ATP per sugar molecule. Others use other molecules (like nitrate or sulfate) instead of oxygen (anaerobic respiration) and get less ATP. Others do fermentation, which does not use oxygen and produces much less ATP, but allows them to live where oxygen is absent.

Understanding bacterial metabolism helps us understand how bacteria cause disease, how they recycle nutrients in ecosystems, and how we can use them to make medicines, fuels, and other products.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing the four nutritional types with examples: cyanobacteria (photoautotroph), purple bacteria (photoheterotroph), nitrifying bacteria (chemoautotroph), E. coli (chemoheterotroph)
introduction_image.caption: Four nutritional types: Photoautotrophs (cyanobacteria), Photoheterotrophs (purple bacteria), Chemoautotrophs (nitrifying bacteria), Chemoheterotrophs (E. coli). Each uses a different combination of energy and carbon sources.

key_concept.definition: Bacteria are classified by their energy source (light vs. chemicals) and carbon source (CO₂ vs. organic compounds). The four groups are: photoautotrophs, photoheterotrophs, chemoautotrophs, and chemoheterotrophs.
key_concept.context: Energy sources: light (phototrophs) or chemical compounds (chemotrophs). Carbon sources: CO₂ (autotrophs) or organic compounds (heterotrophs). Bacteria can switch between pathways depending on conditions. Many are versatile—E. coli can use oxygen when it is available, or switch to fermentation without oxygen.

image.alt: A table showing the four nutritional types with energy and carbon sources and examples
image.caption: Nutritional classification: Photoautotrophs (light + CO₂), Photoheterotrophs (light + organic), Chemoautotrophs (chemicals + CO₂), Chemoheterotrophs (organic + organic). Bacteria use all four strategies.

sections.key_concept: Bacteria get energy in three main ways: photosynthesis (using light), respiration (using oxygen or other molecules), and fermentation (without oxygen). Each produces different amounts of ATP.

classifications.value: Understanding phototrophy explains how some bacteria make energy from light, like plants.
characteristics: Photosynthesis: Using light energy to make ATP and NADPH. Some bacteria produce oxygen (oxygenic photosynthesis), others do not (anoxygenic).
Oxygenic photosynthesis: Uses water (H₂O) as electron donor, produces oxygen. Found in cyanobacteria. Has two photosystems (PSI and PSII).
Anoxygenic photosynthesis: Uses other electron donors (H₂S, H₂, organic compounds), no oxygen produced. Found in purple and green bacteria. Has one photosystem.
Calvin cycle: The pathway that fixes CO₂ into organic carbon. Used by many photosynthetic bacteria and plants.
Light-harvesting complexes: Structures that capture light and transfer energy to reaction centers.
classifications.behavior:
Photosynthetic bacteria have been around for billions of years. Cyanobacteria were the first to produce oxygen. They changed the Earth's atmosphere from anoxic (no oxygen) to oxygen-rich, allowing the evolution of animals.

Oxygenic photosynthesis works like this: Light hits photosystem II, exciting electrons. The electrons are replaced by splitting water, releasing oxygen. The electrons travel through an electron transport chain, pumping protons and making ATP. They then go to photosystem I, get excited again, and reduce NADP⁺ to NADPH. The ATP and NADPH are used in the Calvin cycle to fix CO₂ into sugar.

Anoxygenic photosynthesis is simpler. It has only one photosystem. Electrons come from hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) or other donors, not water. No oxygen is produced. These bacteria are found in environments with light but no oxygen—like deep in microbial mats, or in sulfur springs. They often store sulfur granules inside their cells.

Some bacteria are photoheterotrophs. They use light for energy but still need organic carbon. They get ATP from cyclic photophosphorylation (electrons cycle back to the photosystem, making ATP but no NADPH). They must get carbon from organic compounds.

examples.explanation:
Cyanobacteria are the only bacteria that perform oxygenic photosynthesis. They are often called blue-green algae, but they are bacteria. They were the first organisms to produce oxygen. About 2.4 billion years ago, cyanobacteria filled the atmosphere with oxygen, causing the Great Oxidation Event. This allowed the evolution of oxygen-breathing life.

How do they do it? Cyanobacteria have chlorophyll a (like plants) and phycobilisomes (light-harvesting structures). They have two photosystems. Photosystem II splits water, releasing oxygen. The electrons go through an electron transport chain, making ATP. Then they go to photosystem I, where they are re-energized and reduce NADP⁺ to NADPH. The ATP and NADPH are used to fix CO₂ into sugar in the Calvin cycle.

Cyanobacteria also fix nitrogen. Some have special cells called heterocysts that protect nitrogenase (the enzyme that fixes nitrogen) from oxygen. This allows them to grow in waters with little nitrogen.

Cyanobacteria are important in oceans, lakes, and soils. They are the base of many food chains. Some can form harmful blooms (red tides) when nutrients are high.

image.alt: Cyanobacteria cell with thylakoids, photosystems, and Calvin cycle
image.caption: Cyanobacteria: Use two photosystems (PSII splits water, produces O₂; PSI reduces NADP⁺). ATP and NADPH power Calvin cycle. Heterocysts fix nitrogen away from oxygen.

examples.explanation:
Purple sulfur bacteria live in sulfur springs, salt marshes, and deep in lakes where light reaches but oxygen does not. They use hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) as an electron donor instead of water. They do not produce oxygen.

They have a single photosystem. Light excites electrons, which travel through an electron transport chain, pumping protons and making ATP. The electrons then return to the reaction center (cyclic photophosphorylation). This makes ATP but no NADPH. To get NADPH, they must use energy to run electrons backward (reverse electron flow).

Hydrogen sulfide is oxidized to sulfur, which is stored inside the cell as granules. Later, the sulfur can be further oxidized to sulfate.

Purple sulfur bacteria are important in the sulfur cycle. They convert toxic hydrogen sulfide into less toxic sulfur and sulfate. They are also used in wastewater treatment to remove sulfide.

image.alt: Purple sulfur bacterium with single photosystem, H₂S oxidation, and sulfur granules
image.caption: Purple sulfur bacteria: Single photosystem, H₂S as electron donor → sulfur granules. Cyclic photophosphorylation makes ATP; reverse electron flow makes NADPH. No oxygen produced.

classifications.value: Understanding chemotrophy explains how bacteria get energy from chemicals—from sugar to ammonia to iron.
characteristics: Chemoautotrophs: Get energy from inorganic chemicals (NH₃, H₂S, Fe²⁺, H₂) and carbon from CO₂.
Chemoheterotrophs: Get both energy and carbon from organic compounds (sugars, fats, proteins).
Aerobic respiration: Uses oxygen as the final electron acceptor. Produces the most ATP.
Anaerobic respiration: Uses other molecules (nitrate, sulfate, iron) as electron acceptors. Produces less ATP.
Fermentation: No electron transport chain. ATP made by substrate-level phosphorylation. Produces much less ATP.
classifications.behavior:
Chemoautotrophs are the masters of extreme environments. They live where there is no light and no organic carbon. They get energy from chemicals that would be useless to most organisms.

Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas) oxidize ammonia (NH₃) to nitrite (NO₂⁻). They use the energy to fix CO₂. Other bacteria (Nitrobacter) oxidize nitrite to nitrate (NO₃⁻). Together, they convert ammonia from decomposing organic matter into nitrate, which plants can use. This is an essential part of the nitrogen cycle.

Sulfur-oxidizing bacteria live near deep-sea vents. They oxidize hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) from the vents to sulfur or sulfate. They fix CO₂ into organic carbon. They are the base of the food chain in vent ecosystems—tube worms and other animals live on them.

Iron-oxidizing bacteria (Acidithiobacillus) oxidize iron (Fe²⁺) to iron (Fe³⁺). They live in acidic mine drainage and can cause environmental problems by releasing toxic metals. But they are also used to extract metals from ores (bioleaching).

Chemoheterotrophs are the most common bacteria. They include decomposers, pathogens, and gut bacteria. They break down organic compounds to get energy and carbon. They use aerobic respiration when oxygen is available, and switch to anaerobic respiration or fermentation when it is not.

examples.process: Nitrifying Bacteria: Making Fertilizer from Ammonia
examples.explanation:
When plants and animals die, their proteins are broken down into ammonia (NH₃). Ammonia is toxic to plants in high amounts, but plants need nitrogen to grow. Nitrifying bacteria convert ammonia into nitrate (NO₃⁻), which plants can use as fertilizer.

Ammonia oxidizers (Nitrosomonas) oxidize NH₃ to NO₂⁻. They use the electrons to make ATP and to reduce NAD⁺ to NADH. They need oxygen for this reaction. They then fix CO₂ into organic carbon using the Calvin cycle.

Nitrite oxidizers (Nitrobacter) oxidize NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻. They also use the energy to fix CO₂. Together, these bacteria convert toxic ammonia into usable nitrate.

Nitrification is important in agriculture. Farmers add ammonia-based fertilizers to soil, but plants cannot use ammonia directly. Nitrifying bacteria convert it to nitrate, which plants can take up. However, nitrate is easily washed out of soil, so timing matters.

image.alt: Nitrification pathway: ammonia → nitrite → nitrate, with Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter
image.caption: Nitrification: Ammonia oxidizers (Nitrosomonas) convert NH₃ → NO₂⁻; nitrite oxidizers (Nitrobacter) convert NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻. Both use energy to fix CO₂. Essential for nitrogen cycle.

examples.explanation:
E. coli lives in your gut. It is a chemoheterotroph—it gets energy and carbon from organic compounds. It can use many different sugars, but it prefers glucose. It is also versatile in how it makes ATP.

When oxygen is present, E. coli uses aerobic respiration. It breaks down glucose to CO₂ and H₂O, producing about 38 ATP per glucose. This is very efficient.

When oxygen is absent, E. coli can switch to anaerobic respiration. It uses nitrate (NO₃⁻) instead of oxygen as an electron acceptor. This produces less ATP, but still more than fermentation.

If neither oxygen nor nitrate is available, E. coli does fermentation. It breaks down glucose to a mixture of acids (lactate, acetate) and gases (CO₂, H₂). This produces only 2 ATP per glucose, but it allows E. coli to grow without oxygen.

This flexibility is why E. coli can live in your gut (where oxygen is low) and also survive outside. It can also use many different carbon sources—glucose, lactose, amino acids—depending on what is available.

image.alt: E. coli metabolism: aerobic respiration (O₂), anaerobic respiration (NO₃⁻), fermentation
image.caption: E. coli: With O₂ → aerobic respiration (high ATP). With NO₃⁻ → anaerobic respiration (moderate ATP). Without O₂/NO₃⁻ → fermentation (low ATP). Flexible metabolism allows survival in gut and outside.

classifications.type: Fermentation vs. Respiration: Making ATP Without Oxygen
classifications.value: Understanding fermentation and anaerobic respiration explains how bacteria live in places without oxygen.
characteristics: Aerobic respiration: Oxygen is final electron acceptor. Produces up to 38 ATP per glucose. Requires oxygen.
Anaerobic respiration: Other molecules (nitrate, sulfate, iron) are electron acceptors. Produces less ATP than aerobic, more than fermentation.
Fermentation: No electron transport chain. Organic molecules serve as both electron donor and acceptor. Produces only 2 ATP per glucose. No oxygen required.
Electron transport chain: Uses energy from electron flow to pump protons across membrane, creating proton gradient that drives ATP synthesis.
ATP synthase: The enzyme that makes ATP from the proton gradient.
classifications.behavior:
Respiration uses an electron transport chain to make ATP. Electrons from food (like glucose) are passed through a series of carriers, eventually reaching a final electron acceptor. As electrons flow, protons are pumped across the membrane, creating a gradient. ATP synthase uses the gradient to make ATP. The more energy the final electron acceptor can capture, the more ATP is made.

Aerobic respiration uses oxygen (O₂) as the final electron acceptor. Oxygen has a high affinity for electrons, so a lot of energy is released. This yields about 38 ATP per glucose.

Anaerobic respiration uses other molecules as electron acceptors. Nitrate (NO₃⁻), sulfate (SO₄²⁻), and iron (Fe³⁺) are common. These acceptors have lower energy than oxygen, so less ATP is made—about 10-20 ATP per glucose.

Fermentation does not use an electron transport chain. Instead, electrons from NADH are passed directly to an organic molecule, regenerating NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue. This produces only 2 ATP per glucose (from glycolysis). But it allows bacteria to grow in places with no oxygen and no other electron acceptors.

Many bacteria can switch between respiration and fermentation depending on conditions. E. coli prefers oxygen, but can switch to nitrate respiration if oxygen is low, and to fermentation if no other acceptors are available.

examples.process: Denitrification: Removing Nitrate from Soil and Water
examples.explanation:
Denitrification is a type of anaerobic respiration. Bacteria use nitrate (NO₃⁻) as an electron acceptor, reducing it to nitrogen gas (N₂). This is important in the nitrogen cycle—it returns nitrogen to the atmosphere.

Denitrifying bacteria (like Paracoccus) live in soils and sediments with low oxygen. They use organic compounds as electron donors and nitrate as electron acceptor. The process has several steps: NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ → NO → N₂O → N₂. Each step is catalyzed by a different enzyme.

Denitrification is important in agriculture. Too much nitrate in soil can be lost to the atmosphere as N₂. This reduces fertilizer efficiency. It also produces nitrous oxide (N₂O), a greenhouse gas. Farmers try to reduce denitrification by timing fertilizer application and avoiding waterlogged soils.

Denitrification is also used in wastewater treatment. Bacteria remove nitrate from water, preventing eutrophication (algal blooms) in rivers and lakes.

image.alt: Denitrification pathway: NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ → NO → N₂O → N₂, with enzymes
image.caption: Denitrification: Bacteria reduce NO₃⁻ to N₂ gas. Returns nitrogen to atmosphere. Important in agriculture (loss of fertilizer) and wastewater treatment (removes nitrate).

examples.explanation:
Lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus) ferment sugars to lactic acid. This is how yogurt, cheese, and sauerkraut are made.

Lactobacillus breaks down glucose to pyruvate via glycolysis, making 2 ATP and 2 NADH. It then uses the NADH to reduce pyruvate to lactate. This regenerates NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue. No oxygen is used. No electron transport chain is involved. The only ATP comes from glycolysis.

The lactic acid produced lowers the pH, which preserves the food and gives yogurt its tangy taste. The acid also prevents the growth of harmful bacteria.

Lactic acid bacteria are also important in your gut. They help digest food and prevent the growth of pathogens. Some are used as probiotics—live bacteria that are good for your health.

image.alt: Lactic acid fermentation: glucose → pyruvate (2 ATP, 2 NADH) → lactate (regenerates NAD⁺)
image.caption: Lactic acid fermentation: Glucose → 2 pyruvate (2 ATP, 2 NADH) → 2 lactate (regenerates NAD⁺). No oxygen, no ETC, only 2 ATP per glucose. Used in yogurt, cheese, sauerkraut.

principles.statement: Bacteria prefer pathways that give the most ATP. Aerobic respiration gives the most, anaerobic respiration gives less, and fermentation gives the least. When oxygen is available, bacteria use it. When it is not, they use other strategies.
principles.application:
Aerobic respiration yields about 38 ATP per glucose. Anaerobic respiration yields about 10-20 ATP. Fermentation yields only 2 ATP. This is why bacteria with oxygen grow much faster than without.

E. coli in your gut: If oxygen is present, it will use aerobic respiration. But your gut is mostly anaerobic, so E. coli uses nitrate respiration or fermentation. It grows slower, but it survives.

This principle explains why we need to keep oxygen away from certain bacteria. Clostridium botulinum (causes botulism) is an obligate anaerobe—it cannot grow in oxygen. It ferments and produces a deadly toxin. Canned foods are heated to kill spores, and the can is sealed to keep oxygen out? Actually, botulism spores survive heating; but the can is sealed to keep oxygen out? Wait—Clostridium grows without oxygen. So sealing the can creates anaerobic conditions that allow it to grow. That is why canned foods must be heated to kill spores—the can is sealed, creating an anaerobic environment that Clostridium loves.

This principle also explains why we use oxygen to treat wounds. Oxygen kills anaerobic bacteria that cause infections in deep wounds.
principles.example: In a pond, oxygen is present near the surface but absent at the bottom. Aerobic bacteria live near the surface, where they can use oxygen for respiration. Anaerobic bacteria (like sulfate reducers) live at the bottom, where they use sulfate as an electron acceptor. Fermenters live in the mud, where no other acceptors are available. Each has found a niche where its energy strategy works.
principles.analogy: Energy yield is like money. Aerobic respiration is like working a high-paying job—you get a lot of ATP per glucose. Anaerobic respiration is like a lower-paying job—still decent, but not as good. Fermentation is like a minimum-wage job—you barely get enough to survive. Bacteria will take the highest-paying job available.

image.alt: Comparison of ATP yield: aerobic respiration (38 ATP), anaerobic respiration (10-20 ATP), fermentation (2 ATP)
image.caption: Energy yield: Aerobic respiration (O₂) → 38 ATP; Anaerobic respiration (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻) → 10-20 ATP; Fermentation → 2 ATP. Bacteria use the highest-yield pathway available.

principles.statement: Bacteria drive the cycles of carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and iron on Earth. Without them, nutrients would be locked up in dead matter and unavailable to other life.
principles.application:
When a plant or animal dies, its body is full of carbon, nitrogen, and other nutrients. Bacteria break it down. Decomposers (chemoheterotrophs) use the organic matter for energy and carbon, releasing CO₂ back to the air and NH₃ to the soil. Nitrifying bacteria then convert NH₃ to NO₃⁻, which plants can use. Denitrifying bacteria convert NO₃⁻ back to N₂, completing the cycle.

Sulfur bacteria cycle sulfur. Some oxidize H₂S to S⁰ or SO₄²⁻. Others reduce SO₄²⁻ to H₂S. Iron bacteria cycle iron between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺.

Without bacteria, dead matter would pile up. Nutrients would be locked in corpses. Plants would starve. The cycles of life would stop. Bacteria are the great recyclers of the biosphere.
principles.example: In a forest, leaves fall to the ground. Bacteria and fungi break down the leaves, releasing CO₂ to the air and NH₃ to the soil. Nitrifying bacteria convert NH₃ to NO₃⁻. Plants take up the NO₃⁻ to make new leaves. The cycle continues. Without decomposers, the forest would be buried in leaves, and plants would run out of nitrogen.
principles.analogy: Bacteria are like the recycling center of the Earth. They take dead stuff and turn it into stuff that living things can use. Without them, the planet would be a landfill.

image.alt: Diagram of the nitrogen cycle with bacteria: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification
image.caption: Nutrient cycling: Bacteria fix N₂ (nitrogen fixation), convert NH₃ to NO₃⁻ (nitrification), and convert NO₃⁻ to N₂ (denitrification). Without bacteria, nutrients would be locked in dead matter.

principles.principle: Bacteria Adapt Their Metabolism to the Environment
principles.statement: Bacteria sense what nutrients are available and turn on the pathways they need. They do not waste energy making enzymes for food that is not there.
principles.application:
E. coli has genes for using many different sugars. But if glucose is present, it will not waste energy making enzymes for lactose. This is called catabolite repression. When glucose is high, cAMP levels are low, and the activator protein (CRP) cannot bind. The genes for lactose utilization are turned off. When glucose runs out, cAMP rises, CRP activates the lactose genes, and E. coli starts using lactose.

Bacteria also sense oxygen. When oxygen is present, they turn on the genes for aerobic respiration. When oxygen is absent, they turn on genes for anaerobic respiration or fermentation. This is controlled by regulators like Fnr (which senses oxygen) and ArcA (which senses the redox state of the cell).

This ability to sense and respond to the environment allows bacteria to thrive in changing conditions. They do not waste energy on pathways they do not need.
principles.example: When E. coli is growing in a flask with both glucose and lactose, it uses glucose first. The cells grow fast, using the high-energy sugar. When glucose runs out, there is a lag while the cells turn on the lactose genes. Then they grow again, using lactose. This diauxic growth (two phases) shows how bacteria prioritize energy sources.
principles.analogy: Bacteria are like smart cooks. They will use the best ingredients first. If they have steak (glucose) and leftovers (lactose), they eat the steak first. When the steak is gone, they turn on the stove and heat up the leftovers. They do not waste energy heating up leftovers while steak is still on the plate.

image.alt: Diauxic growth curve: first phase (glucose), lag, second phase (lactose)
image.caption: Diauxic growth: E. coli uses glucose first (high-energy sugar). When glucose is gone, it turns on lactose genes and grows again. Bacteria prioritize best energy sources.

components.definition: Bacterial metabolism has three key components: catabolism (breaking down molecules for energy), anabolism (building cell components), and regulation (controlling which pathways are active).
components.interpretation: Catabolism breaks down food to make ATP and building blocks. Anabolism uses ATP and building blocks to make cell parts. Regulation ensures that the right pathways are active at the right time. If catabolism is too fast, the cell wastes energy. If anabolism is too fast, it runs out of building blocks. Regulation balances the two.

image.alt: Diagram showing catabolism (breaking down), anabolism (building up), and regulation (control)
image.caption: Metabolism components: Catabolism (breakdown → ATP + building blocks), Anabolism (building blocks → cell components), Regulation (controls flow). Balance is essential.

categories.description: The electron transport chain (ETC) is how bacteria get most of their ATP. Electrons from food are passed through a series of carriers, pumping protons across the membrane. The protons flow back through ATP synthase, making ATP.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
The ETC is located in the cell membrane. It has several protein complexes. NADH gives electrons to the first complex (NADH dehydrogenase). The electrons are passed to quinones, then to other complexes, and finally to the terminal electron acceptor (oxygen, nitrate, etc.). As electrons move, protons are pumped out of the cell. This creates a proton gradient (more protons outside than inside). ATP synthase uses the flow of protons back into the cell to make ATP.

Different bacteria have different ETC components. Some have more complexes than others. The number of protons pumped per electron determines how much ATP is made. Oxygen is the best terminal electron acceptor because it has a high affinity for electrons. Nitrate gives less energy, sulfate even less.

examples.explanation:
ATP synthase is a tiny motor. It sits in the cell membrane. Protons flow through it, making a rotor spin. The spinning causes a part of the enzyme to change shape, squeezing ADP and phosphate together to make ATP. It is like a hydroelectric dam—water (protons) flowing through a turbine (ATP synthase) generates power (ATP).

ATP synthase is found in bacteria, mitochondria, and chloroplasts. It is one of the most conserved enzymes in nature. It has two parts: the F₀ part (in the membrane) where protons flow, and the F₁ part (on the inside) where ATP is made. The rotor can spin at thousands of revolutions per minute.

image.alt: ATP synthase structure with rotor and stator, proton flow driving ATP synthesis
image.caption: ATP synthase: Proton flow through F₀ rotates the rotor; F₁ changes shape, squeezing ADP + Pi → ATP. Found in bacteria, mitochondria, chloroplasts.

worked_examples.title: How Much ATP Does a Bacterium Get from Glucose?
worked_examples.problem: E. coli can metabolize glucose in three ways: (a) aerobic respiration (with oxygen), (b) anaerobic respiration (with nitrate), and (c) fermentation. (1) How many ATP are made per glucose by each pathway? (2) Why does E. coli prefer oxygen when it is available? (3) Why does fermentation make so much less ATP?

step_by_step.calculation: Glycolysis breaks one glucose into two pyruvate. It uses 2 ATP at the start and makes 4 ATP later, for a net of 2 ATP. It also makes 2 NADH.

The 2 NADH from glycolysis go to the ETC. Each NADH can make about 3 ATP (in bacteria, about 2-3). The 2 pyruvate are converted to acetyl-CoA (2 NADH). The 2 acetyl-CoA go through the TCA cycle, making 6 NADH, 2 FADH₂, and 2 ATP. Total: 10 NADH (2+2+6) and 2 FADH₂. Each NADH gives ~3 ATP (30 ATP), each FADH₂ gives ~2 ATP (4 ATP). Plus 4 ATP from substrate-level (2 from glycolysis, 2 from TCA). Total ~38 ATP.

Nitrate respiration uses the same number of NADH and FADH₂, but nitrate gives less energy than oxygen. Each NADH makes about 2 ATP, each FADH₂ about 1.5 ATP. Total: 10 NADH × 2 = 20; 2 FADH₂ × 1.5 = 3; plus 4 substrate-level = 27 ATP. Actual yields vary, but less than aerobic.
Fermentation uses only glycolysis. The 2 NADH are used to reduce pyruvate to lactate or other products, regenerating NAD⁺. No ETC, no additional ATP. Total ATP = 2 (from glycolysis).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Aerobic respiration: ~38 ATP; Nitrate respiration: ~27 ATP; Fermentation: 2 ATP. (2) E. coli prefers oxygen because it gets the most ATP, allowing faster growth. (3) Fermentation makes only 2 ATP because it does not use the ETC; the energy in NADH is not captured, and pyruvate is not fully oxidized. This shows why bacteria with oxygen grow faster than those without.
worked_examples.concept_applied: ATP yield depends on the electron acceptor. Oxygen gives the most energy, nitrate less, fermentation none from ETC. Bacteria use the best acceptor available to maximize growth.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Glucose Metabolism in Bacteria
sections.introduction: Glucose is a common food for bacteria. How they break it down depends on oxygen. Understanding this flow reveals how bacteria get energy.

process_flow.description: Glucose enters the cell and is phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate.
process_flow.mechanism: The phosphotransferase system (PTS) transports glucose and adds a phosphate at the same time. The phosphate comes from phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP). This saves ATP and also sends signals about glucose availability.
process_flow.result: Glucose-6-phosphate inside the cell; ready for catabolism.
applications: PTS links transport to regulation; high glucose = low cAMP, represses other sugar genes.

image.alt: PTS system: glucose in, glucose-6-phosphate out, PEP to pyruvate
image.caption: Step 1: Glucose enters via PTS, phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate. Uses PEP, not ATP. Also signals glucose availability.

process_flow.description: Glucose-6-phosphate is broken down to two pyruvate, making ATP and NADH.
process_flow.mechanism: Glycolysis has 10 steps. Glucose-6-phosphate → fructose-6-phosphate → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate → two trioses → two pyruvate. Net: 2 ATP, 2 NADH.
applications: Glycolysis is universal; works with or without oxygen.

image.alt: Glycolysis steps from glucose to pyruvate, with ATP and NADH yields
image.caption: Step 2: Glycolysis breaks glucose to 2 pyruvate. Net: 2 ATP, 2 NADH. Occurs in cytoplasm; does not require oxygen.

process_flow.description: Pyruvate is processed depending on oxygen availability.
process_flow.mechanism: If oxygen is present: pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → TCA cycle → NADH/FADH₂ → ETC → many ATP. If oxygen is absent but other acceptors (nitrate) available: similar but less ATP. If no acceptors: pyruvate → lactate, ethanol, or other products (fermentation), regenerating NAD⁺, only 2 ATP from glycolysis.
process_flow.result: High ATP (aerobic), moderate ATP (anaerobic respiration), or low ATP (fermentation).
applications: E. coli switches based on oxygen; obligate anaerobes cannot use oxygen.

image.alt: Pyruvate branch: with O₂ → TCA/ETC (many ATP); with NO₃⁻ → anaerobic respiration (less ATP); without acceptors → fermentation (2 ATP)
image.caption: Step 3: Pyruvate fate depends on oxygen and other acceptors. With O₂ → TCA + ETC (high ATP). With NO₃⁻ → anaerobic respiration (moderate ATP). Without acceptors → fermentation (low ATP).

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Using Bacteria for Biotechnology
sections.definition: Bacteria are used to make medicines, fuels, and chemicals. By engineering their metabolism, we can make them produce things we want.

process_types.energy_profile: Redirecting carbon from growth to product can reduce growth rate but increase yield.
process_types.direction: Glucose → normal metabolism → biomass → engineered pathway → product
process_types.function: Make bacteria produce fuels (ethanol, butanol) or chemicals (plastic precursors) instead of just growing.
process_types.energy_source: Glucose is the feedstock; bacteria convert it to product using their metabolic machinery.

primary_example.explanation:
Isobutanol is a better biofuel than ethanol. It has more energy and does not absorb water. E. coli does not normally make isobutanol, but scientists engineered it to do so.

The pathway starts with pyruvate. Scientists added genes from other bacteria to convert pyruvate to 2-ketoisovalerate, then to isobutyraldehyde, then to isobutanol. They also knocked out competing pathways (like lactate and ethanol fermentation) so more carbon goes to isobutanol.

They also had to balance NADPH levels. The pathway uses NADPH, so they added a transhydrogenase to convert NADH to NADPH.

The engineered E. coli can make over 50 grams of isobutanol per liter—enough to be useful. This shows how understanding metabolism lets us turn bacteria into chemical factories.

image.alt: Engineered isobutanol pathway in E. coli, with competing pathways knocked out
image.caption: Isobutanol production: Engineered pathway converts pyruvate → isobutanol. Competing pathways (lactate, ethanol) knocked out. Transhydrogenase balances NADPH. E. coli becomes a biofuel factory.

image.alt: Diagram of metabolic engineering: add genes, knock out genes, balance cofactors
image.caption: Metabolic engineering: Add pathway genes, knock out competing pathways, balance cofactors (NADH/NADPH). Transform bacteria into factories for fuels, medicines, and chemicals.

process_types.energy_profile: Bacteria use organic waste as food, converting it to CO₂ and clean water. This costs energy but cleans the environment.
process_types.direction: Wastewater (organic matter) → bacteria → CO₂ + H₂O + N₂ → clean water
process_types.function: Remove organic matter, nitrogen, and phosphorus from sewage before releasing water to rivers.
process_types.energy_source: Organic matter in sewage provides energy for bacteria; aeration provides oxygen.

primary_example.explanation:
In a wastewater treatment plant, sewage is mixed with bacteria in large tanks. Air is bubbled through (aeration), providing oxygen for aerobic bacteria. These bacteria break down organic matter into CO₂ and water.

The bacteria form clumps (flocs) that settle out in a settling tank. Some of the settled bacteria are returned to the aeration tank (activated sludge), and the rest are removed as waste. The treated water is then disinfected and released.

To remove nitrogen, the plant uses nitrifying bacteria (convert NH₃ to NO₃⁻) in the aerobic tank, then denitrifying bacteria (convert NO₃⁻ to N₂) in an anaerobic tank. The N₂ gas escapes to the atmosphere.

This process is one of the most important uses of bacteria. It cleans the water we return to rivers, preventing pollution and disease.

image.alt: Wastewater treatment plant: aeration tank (organic removal, nitrification), settling tank, anaerobic tank (denitrification)
image.caption: Wastewater treatment: Aerobic bacteria break down organic matter; nitrifying bacteria convert NH₃ → NO₃⁻; denitrifying bacteria convert NO₃⁻ → N₂. Bacteria clean water naturally.

image.alt: Activated sludge process: sewage in, aeration, settling, clean water out
image.caption: Activated sludge: Bacteria consume organic matter in aerated tanks. They settle out, some returned (activated sludge), rest removed. Clean water discharged.

sections.relationship: Metabolic engineering and wastewater treatment both use our understanding of bacterial metabolism. In engineering, we redirect metabolism to make products. In treatment, we harness natural metabolism to clean the environment. Both show how bacteria can serve human needs.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding bacterial metabolism. Knowing these terms will help you describe how bacteria get energy and carbon.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in bacterial metabolism. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss how bacteria grow, how they get energy, and how they are used in biotechnology.

image.alt: A diagram showing key metabolism terms: photoautotroph, chemoheterotroph, respiration, fermentation, ETC, ATP
image.caption: Key metabolism terms: Knowing these helps you understand how bacteria get energy and carbon.

key_properties.explanation: An organism that gets energy from light and carbon from CO₂. Examples: cyanobacteria, plants. Use photosynthesis to make organic compounds.
An organism that gets energy from inorganic chemicals (like ammonia, hydrogen sulfide) and carbon from CO₂. Examples: nitrifying bacteria, sulfur-oxidizing bacteria.
An organism that gets both energy and carbon from organic compounds. Examples: animals, fungi, most bacteria (E. coli, pathogens, decomposers).
Using oxygen as the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. Produces the most ATP (about 38 per glucose).
Using other molecules (nitrate, sulfate, iron) as electron acceptors. Produces less ATP than aerobic respiration but more than fermentation.
No electron transport chain. Organic molecules serve as both electron donor and acceptor. Produces only 2 ATP per glucose. Examples: lactic acid fermentation (yogurt), alcoholic fermentation (beer).
A series of proteins in the membrane that pass electrons from donors to acceptors, pumping protons and creating a gradient. The gradient drives ATP synthesis.
The enzyme that makes ATP from the proton gradient. Protons flow through it, causing a rotor to spin, which drives ATP synthesis.
The pathway that breaks glucose into two pyruvate. Produces 2 ATP and 2 NADH. Does not require oxygen.
The pathway that oxidizes acetyl-CoA to CO₂, producing NADH, FADH₂, and ATP. Occurs in the cytoplasm in bacteria (mitochondria in eukaryotes).
The pathway that fixes CO₂ into organic carbon. Uses ATP and NADPH from photosynthesis. Found in plants, algae, and cyanobacteria.
The process of converting ammonia (NH₃) to nitrate (NO₃⁻) by bacteria. First step: NH₃ → NO₂⁻ (Nitrosomonas); second step: NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (Nitrobacter).
The process of converting nitrate (NO₃⁻) to nitrogen gas (N₂) by bacteria. Returns nitrogen to the atmosphere. Occurs in low-oxygen soils and sediments.
When glucose is present, bacteria repress genes for using other sugars. They use glucose first because it gives the most energy.
Two-phase growth: first on glucose, then on another sugar. There is a lag between phases while bacteria turn on genes for the second sugar.
A bacterium that requires oxygen to grow. Cannot survive without it. Examples: Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
A bacterium that cannot grow in the presence of oxygen. Oxygen is toxic to them. Examples: Clostridium botulinum, Bacteroides.
A bacterium that can grow with or without oxygen. Prefers oxygen (more ATP) but can switch to fermentation or anaerobic respiration when oxygen is absent. Example: E. coli.

summary:
Bacteria are incredibly diverse in how they get energy and carbon. They are classified by energy source (light vs. chemicals) and carbon source (CO₂ vs. organic compounds). Photoautotrophs (cyanobacteria) use light and CO₂, producing oxygen. Photoheterotrophs use light but need organic carbon. Chemoautotrophs use inorganic chemicals (ammonia, hydrogen sulfide) and CO₂. Chemoheterotrophs (most bacteria) use organic compounds for both energy and carbon.

Energy generation happens in three ways. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen as the final electron acceptor and produces the most ATP (about 38 per glucose). Anaerobic respiration uses other acceptors (nitrate, sulfate) and produces less ATP. Fermentation uses no electron transport chain and produces only 2 ATP per glucose. Bacteria choose the highest-yield pathway available.

Bacteria drive global nutrient cycles. Decomposers break down dead matter, releasing CO₂ and NH₃. Nitrifying bacteria convert NH₃ to NO₃⁻, which plants use. Denitrifying bacteria convert NO₃⁻ back to N₂, completing the cycle. Without bacteria, nutrients would be locked in dead matter.

Bacteria regulate their metabolism to match the environment. When glucose is present, they repress genes for other sugars (catabolite repression). They sense oxygen and switch between respiration and fermentation. This flexibility allows them to thrive in changing conditions.

Understanding bacterial metabolism helps us use bacteria in biotechnology. We engineer E. coli to make biofuels (isobutanol) and medicines. We use bacteria in wastewater treatment to remove organic matter and nitrogen. Bacteria are not just germs—they are essential for life on Earth and powerful tools for human needs.
